EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large MCS moved across Middle Tennessee during the evening hours of August 16 into the early morning hours of August 17, producing widespread wind damage and some large hail. Tens of thousands of people were left without power and two people were injured, making this the most damaging severe weather event in the summer of 2012.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were snapped and uprooted with power lines blown down all around the Siegel Road area of north Murfreesboro. Spotter measured a wind gust of 76 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the Mid South during the evening hours on October 5, 2012. Numerous showers and a few thunderstorms developed along and behind the front. A couple of the storms became severe with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down two large trees on South Parkway. Several other large trees and limbs were also knocked down across Midtown. 15,000 homes and businesses lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level low pressure system approached southern and eastern portions of Middle Tennessee during the afternoon through evening hours on Monday, September 17th, low level wind fields strengthened. Along with increased moisture and instability across these portions of the mid state also, severe thunderstorms developed, resulting in thunderstorm wind damage, along with several funnel cloud reports, including an EF0 tornado in Bedford County, impacting several counties in the mid state. Associated impacts of flash flooding events lasted even into the early morning hours on Tuesday, September 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total rainfall accumulation of 5.11 inches was reported in Minor Hill. This rainfall amount resulted in a threat to life and property in Minor Hill. 5.06 inches of total rainfall accumulation was also reported by a CoCoRaHs observer near Anthony Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved into Northeast Arkansas during the evening hours of September 7th, 2012. Showers and thunderstorms developed in response to the disturbance. Some storms became severe producing damaging winds and flash flooding. The storms continued east into West Tennessee during the late evening hours before dying out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were measured at 56 mph with a gust of 69 mph in Halls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved into Northeast Arkansas during the evening hours of September 7th, 2012. Showers and thunderstorms developed in response to the disturbance. Some storms became severe producing damaging winds and flash flooding. The storms continued east into West Tennessee during the late evening hours before dying out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked a tree down onto power lines at the intersection of Little John Road and Old Millington Road near Millington. Another large tree was split near Kirby Whitten Parkway and Ellendale Road in Bartlett. Power poles were also knocked down on Oak Road in Bartlett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved into the Mid-South during the evening hours of July 7th, 2012. Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the disturbance. Some storms became severe producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail fell in Whitehaven.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee during the morning hours, with several reports of wind damage and a few reports of large hail received.  Additional isolated thunderstorms developed later in the day during the evening, producing more wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Manchester Times reported a home at 1374 Lakeshore Drive in the Lakewood Park had half of the roof blown off. Numerous trees all along Lakeshore Drive were snapped or uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moved southwestward through the Tennessee River Valley during the afternoon and evening hours of July 6, 2012. Some thunderstorms across West Tennessee became severe with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds damaged a barn roof and knocked down trees and power poles on Winchester Road east of Paris.  Several trees and power poles were snapped in Paris. A home in Paris suffered minor roof damage, losing some shingles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed all across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours.  Numerous reports of wind damage and a few reports of large hail were received.
Scattered severe thunderstorms developed all across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours.  Numerous reports of wind damage and a few reports of large hail were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager estimated winds at 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed all across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours.  Numerous reports of wind damage and a few reports of large hail were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branch blown down on Music Row.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moved southwestward through the Tennessee River Valley during the afternoon and evening hours of July 6, 2012. Some thunderstorms across West Tennessee became severe with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down a few power lines and several trees east of Bruceton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moved southwestward through the Tennessee River Valley during the afternoon and evening hours of July 6, 2012. Some thunderstorms across West Tennessee became severe with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down several trees south of Milan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee during the morning hours, with several reports of wind damage and a few reports of large hail received.  Additional isolated thunderstorms developed later in the day during the evening, producing more wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail reported on Central Avenue in Springfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee during the late morning to early afternoon hours, producing wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house at 58 Kelsey Drive in the New Prospect community east of Lawrenceburg. The resulting fire caused a total loss to the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot period occurred during the last week of June. Maximum temperatures climbed to 100 degrees or higher on the 28th through 30th in Winchester, the 28th through 30th at Fayetteville, and on the 29th and 30th at Lynchburg.  Even on the Cumberland Plateau, the temperature reached 101 degrees at Sewanee on the 30th.  At Lynchburg, the temperature reached 108 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The maximum temperature reached 100 degrees in Winchester.

Tens of thousands of people were left without power and two people were injured, making this the most damaging severe weather event in the summer of 2012.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Shelter Branch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High temperatures climbed into the 100 to 105 degree range at many locations in southern middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The high temperature in Fayetteville reached 103 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moved southwestward through the Tennessee River Valley during the afternoon and evening hours of July 6, 2012. Some thunderstorms across West Tennessee became severe with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down trees throughout Weakley County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moved southwestward through the Tennessee River Valley during the afternoon and evening hours of July 6, 2012. Some thunderstorms across West Tennessee became severe with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down several trees across North Jackson. A large tree was split and fell on a home on Dearborne Cove in North Jackson. A tree was uprooted and power lines were knocked down on Sleepywood drive. Power lines were also knocked down on Netherwood Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moved southwestward through the Tennessee River Valley during the afternoon and evening hours of July 6, 2012. Some thunderstorms across West Tennessee became severe with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down a few trees near the Walnut Grove community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved into the Mid-South during the evening hours of July 7th, 2012. Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the disturbance. Some storms became severe producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down trees and power lines on Florence Road in Savannah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 5 month old baby died of hyperthermia in Donelson after being left inside a vehicle for 7 to 8 hours on August 7. The high temperature reached 93 degrees at the nearby Nashville International Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 5 month old baby died of hyperthermia in Donelson after being left inside a vehicle for 7 to 8 hours on August 7. The high temperature reached 93 degrees at the nearby Nashville International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low level jet developed in advance of a cold front moving across the Southern Appalachian Mountains during the early morning hours. Isolated severe convection developed resulting in wind damage.  In addition, an EF-0 tornado developed in Blount county near Ellejoy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms blossomed over the Southern Appalachian region due to the presence of a weak upper level impulse and a cool front building south into a moderately unstable air mass.  Warm temperatures aloft limited hail production, but wind damage was widespread during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down on Oak Grove Road three miles northeast of Dandridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable environment along with an approaching weak surface trough dropping through Tennessee ignited several supercell thunderstorms during the mid afternoon  hours across southern middle Tennessee, which dropped south into northern and central Alabama during the late afternoon and evening hours. A couple of the supercell storms produced large hail and isolated wind damage in Lincoln and Moore Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were knocked down on Highway 129 near the Bedford County line.  Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Through the evening hours on Thursday, July 19th, across several Middle Tennessee counties, some of the the scattered to numerous thunderstorms that developed across these counties reached severe thresholds, resulting in several reports of thunderstorm wind damage and lightning strike damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed and a trampoline was flipped over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large MCS moved across Middle Tennessee during the evening hours of August 16 into the early morning hours of August 17, producing widespread wind damage and some large hail. Tens of thousands of people were left without power and two people were injured, making this the most damaging severe weather event in the summer of 2012.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A two foot diameter tree was blown down on Horn Tavern Road at Bahne Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front tracked across east Tennessee during the overnight hours triggering scattered thunderstorms over the area. A few of the storms produced damaging thunderstorm wind and hail as large as quarters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported several trees downed by thunderstorm wind near Lake City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours on Saturday, March 31st, as a surface frontal boundary moved from southern Kentucky into central portions of Middle Tennessee, scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed south of this boundary, aided also by unseasonably warm surface temperatures, but significantly colder air just off the surface and aloft. The severe thunderstorms that developed across this area produced large hail in several counties. Hail reports ranged from nickel to baseball size. Thunderstorm wind damage and flash flooding also occured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some streets were covered with water in Pulaski.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moving directly across Middle Tennessee spawned numerous showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon hours on April 5.  Several reports of large hail and one report of flooding were received.

A large MCS moved southeastward out of Kentucky across northeast portions of Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours on April 26.  Numerous reports of large hail and wind damage with 1 injury were received.  As a cold front moved southward during the afternoon, additional widely scattered multicell and supercell thunderstorms developed and moved southeastward, resulting in numerous additional large hail reports and one EF0 tornado that caused 6 minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inch diameter tree branches blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee during the late morning to early afternoon hours, producing wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down near the Fedex World Service Center at 127 Athens Way north of downtown Nashville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Sunday, May 6th, as an area of showers and thunderstorms moved across western portions of Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours and an area of showers and thunderstorms developed across western and central portions of the mid state during the mid afternoon and lasted through the early evening hours, some thunderstorms reached severe thresholds, with seven different counties experiencing either thunderstorm wind damage or large hail or both. Two homes were also struck by lightning in Williamson County.

A strong storm system moving across the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys caused scattered strong to severe thunderstorms to develop across eastern portions of Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours. Two supercell thunderstorms spawned four tornadoes resulting in a total of 3 fatalities and 7 injuries. Numerous other reports of wind damage and large hail were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Organized convection developed over the Southern Appalachian region in the presence of a very moist and unstable air mass.  Relatively strong winds through a deep portion of the atmosphere helped to produce an extensive outflow boundary resulting in numerous downed trees and significant structural damage to homes and businesses. The powerful thunderstorm complex resulted in four fatalities and 9 injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was killed and four were injured when trees were blown down at the Abrams Creek Campground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Organized convection developed over the Southern Appalachian region in the presence of a very moist and unstable air mass.  Relatively strong winds through a deep portion of the atmosphere helped to produce an extensive outflow boundary resulting in numerous downed trees and significant structural damage to homes and businesses. The powerful thunderstorm complex resulted in four fatalities and 9 injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down on highway 33.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours of June 15. One storm produced wind damage in Rutherford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst caused wind damage along a 1 mile long east to west swath in La Vergne. Two businesses suffered blown in metal doors, loss of metal siding, and broken windows on Sand Hill Road. Trees were blown down on Stones River Road. One tree fell on a house on Scenic Drive. A power line was blown down on Mercury Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a line of showers and thunderstorms moved through Middle Tennessee during the late evening hours of Sunday, January 22nd through the early morning hours of Monday, January 23rd, several thunderstorms reached severe thresholds resulting in damage across 13 different counties in the mid state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 60 mph wind gust was reported by a trained spotter one mile north of mile marker 163 on Interstate 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly during the afternoon hours on Wednesday, March 15th, showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee again in a broad area of increased instability and moisture centered across the southeast U.S aided also by outflow from a mesoscale convective system that moved across southern Kentucky during this time period. With another day of unseasonably warm surface temperatures, but significantly colder air just off the surface and aloft, severe thunderstorms that developed across the mid state produced large hail in several counties. Hail reports ranged from penny to golf ball size. Thunderstorm wind damage and flash flooding also occurred.

From the mid morning hours through the early evening hours on Tuesday, May 29th, across ten counties in Middle Tennessee, as successive lines of showers and thunderstorms moved through during the mid morning hours and through the afternoon hours respectively, some thunderstorms reached severe thresholds producing thunderstorm wind damage. There was also flash flooding in Benton County and a lightning strike in Stewart County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were downed at the intersection of Glenn Raven Road and Turnersville Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that developed across Arkansas and Missouri swept eastward into Middle Tennessee during the afternoon. Several reports of wind damage were received across western portions of the Mid State.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down on Big Sandy River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Organized convection developed over the Southern Appalachian region in the presence of a very moist and unstable air mass.  Relatively strong winds through a deep portion of the atmosphere helped to produce an extensive outflow boundary resulting in numerous downed trees and significant structural damage to homes and businesses. The powerful thunderstorm complex resulted in four fatalities and 9 injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down on Henry Street in Morristown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree and numerous limbs were reported down on Ruggles Ferry Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large MCS moved across Middle Tennessee during the evening hours of August 16 into the early morning hours of August 17, producing widespread wind damage and some large hail. Tens of thousands of people were left without power and two people were injured, making this the most damaging severe weather event in the summer of 2012.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and a power pole were blown down at Waldron Road and Bridgestone Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated moisture and instability permitted routine afternoon thunderstorms to produce severe weather including hail, wind damage, and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rock Bridge Road was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system moved northeast from the Mid Mississippi Valley through the Great Lakes initially driving a warm front northward through the Southern Appalachian region during the afternoon. The associated cold front swept across the area late Friday night. A one hundred knot upper level jet positioned over a 50 knot low level jet resulted in a strongly sheared environment. The intense shear and moderate instability produced an atmosphere conducive to long-lived, discrete, supercell thunderstorms. A total of 9 tornadoes ranging in intensity from EF-0 to EF-3 produced widespread damage and nearly 50 injuries to East Tennessee residents in the path of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported at Etowah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a few thunderstorms persisted across Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours of Saturday, July 7th, heavy rainfall from a thunderstorm across Dickson County resulted in flash flooding. Later in that day during the afternoon through evening hours, isolated to scattered thunderstorms that developed across Dickson and Wayne Counties reached severe thresholds, resulting in thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bank canopy damaged and gas station awning was blown down near the intersection of State Highway 99 and U.S. Highway 64. Numerous trees were also blown down in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moving across the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys caused scattered strong to severe thunderstorms to develop across eastern portions of Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours. Two supercell thunderstorms spawned four tornadoes resulting in a total of 3 fatalities and 7 injuries. Numerous other reports of wind damage and large hail were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inflow into the north side of the EF1 Smithville tornado caused damage along portions of Ponder Road and Ponder Lane. A few homes suffered minor roof damage, and one home lost a porch awning. Numerous trees were snapped or uprooted, with one tree falling onto and destroying a garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of tropical moisture in advance of a slow moving but strong upper level trough tracking east from Texas produced copius amounts of heavy showers and a few thunderstorms over an 18-24 hour period. Rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches were observed at most locations, with some seeing 5 to just over 7 inches. The heaviest amounts were atop the Cumberland Plateau, possibly enhanced by this elevation change.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall occurred, with 4.43 inches measured, near Anderson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure started to weaken across the northern parts of the Mid-South on September 5th. Meanwhile an upper level disturbance moving through the central plains approached an increasingly unstable airmass over the region and triggered numerous thunderstorms across mainly the northern half of the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening of September 5th. Some of the storms were severe with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down several trees across Collierville Arlington Road just north of Collierville. One tree landed on a vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance and associated cold front moved through the Mid-South during the evening hours of October 17, 2012. The cold front interacted with a moist and unstable airmass in place over the region and a line of showers and thunderstorms developed over Eastern Arkansas and then swept east across the Mid-South. Strong winds aloft aided in the development of the line of thunderstorms some of which became severe and produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds produced damage to a mobile home park in Millington. Two mobile homes sustained minor damage. A baseball field dugout roof was blown off in a baseball field adjacent to the mobile home park and tossed 20 feet. A few trees were snapped and uprooted on the west end of the mobile home park. One tree fell on a mobile home trapping the homeowner. He was later freed uninjured. A privacy fence was also blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across Middle Tennessee produced scattered showers and thunderstorms throughout the area. Several reports of large hail were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported via tSpotter in Chapel Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening hours of March 31, 2012. Showers and thunderstorms developed along and out ahead of the front. Some storms became severe producing large hail.

A large MCS moved southeastward out of Kentucky across northeast portions of Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours on April 26.  Numerous reports of large hail and wind damage with 1 injury were received.  As a cold front moved southward during the afternoon, additional widely scattered multicell and supercell thunderstorms developed and moved southeastward, resulting in numerous additional large hail reports and one EF0 tornado that caused 6 minor injuries.

Through the evening hours on Thursday, July 19th, across several Middle Tennessee counties, some of the the scattered to numerous thunderstorms that developed across these counties reached severe thresholds, resulting in several reports of thunderstorm wind damage and lightning strike damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down on Weeping Elm Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of showers and thunderstorms developed and moved across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on Saturday, March 17th, severe thunderstorms developed across six counties in the mid state and resulted in large hail, thunderstorm wind damage, and flash flooding.

Another line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee during the late morning and afternoon hours. A second line of thunderstorms formed over western Kentucky and moved across northern portions of Middle Tennessee in the evening. A few reports of flooding and wind damage were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A retired NWS employee and television media reported minor flooding in and around Hartsville. Several roads had minor flooding including Brummit Road where water was covered the yards of several homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening hours across the region.  One such storm produced isolated wind damage in Lincoln County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were uprooted at the intersection of Pendergrass Road and Highway 275.  Two Bradford pear trees were split in half.  Several small to medium sized limbs were downed along a 2 miles stretch of Pendergrass Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From the mid afternoon through early evening hours on Thursday, July 5th, across several Middle Tennessee counties, some of the the scattered to numerous thunderstorms that developed across these counties did reach severe thresholds, resulting in several reports of thunderstorm wind damage and large hail.

From the mid afternoon through early evening hours on Thursday, July 5th, across several Middle Tennessee counties, some of the the scattered to numerous thunderstorms that developed across these counties did reach severe thresholds, resulting in several reports of thunderstorm wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed across western portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon through early evening hours on Wednesday, March 14th, showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee in a broad area of increased instability and moisture centered across the southeast U.S. With unseasonably warm surface temperatures, but significantly colder air just off the surface and aloft, severe thunderstorms that developed across the mid state produced large hail in several counties. Hail reports ranged from penny to lime size.

As a line of thunderstorms moved through Dickson and Davidson Counties during the afternoon hours on Saturday, November 3rd, a few thunderstorms reached severe thresholds with thunderstorm wind damage, large hail, and even a funnel cloud reported in Dickson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed along State Highway 100 west of Bellevue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From the mid afternoon through evening hours on Wednesday, September 5th, as a line of showers and thunderstorms moved southeastward across Middle Tennessee,   eight counties in Middle Tennessee experienced severe weather, and/or flooding impacts, and/or lightning strike damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Buffalo Road and also on Howard Switch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across western portions of Middle Tennessee during the late afternoon on February 1, then moved eastward during the evening hours. Several reports of large hail were received.

Through the afternoon hours on Saturday, May 19th, scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across central portions of Middle Tennessee. Some of these thunderstorms reached severe thresholds, resulting in either large hail or thunderstorm wind damage or both across a dozen counties in the mid state. Heavy rainfall also resulted in transportation problems in Bedford County.

Through the afternoon hours on Saturday, May 19th, scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across central portions of Middle Tennessee. Some of these thunderstorms reached severe thresholds, resulting in either large hail or thunderstorm wind damage or both across a dozen counties in the mid state. Heavy rainfall also resulted in transportation problems in Bedford County.

As an upper level low pressure system approached southern and eastern portions of Middle Tennessee during the afternoon through evening hours on Monday, September 17th, low level wind fields strengthened. Along with increased moisture and instability across these portions of the mid state also, severe thunderstorms developed, resulting in thunderstorm wind damage, along with several funnel cloud reports, including an EF0 tornado in Bedford County, impacting several counties in the mid state. Associated impacts of flash flooding events lasted even into the early morning hours on Tuesday, September 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One foot of standing water occurred on Davis Lane in Shelbyville. 5.03 inches of total rainfall accumulation was recorded by a CoCoRaHS observer near Wartrace.
